scenario: A client is working through grief after losing their mother six months ago and struggling with returning to normal life.

turns.user_message: I've been thinking about our last session all week. It's been six months since my mom passed, and I still feel like I'm just going through the motions every day.
Everyone keeps telling me I should be 'moving on' by now, but I don't even know what that means. Does it mean forgetting her?
Sometimes I catch myself reaching for my phone to call her, especially when something good happens. Then I remember and it hits me all over again.
I went through some of her belongings last weekend. My sister thinks we should donate her clothes, but I'm not ready for that yet.
PROBE_SLOT
Work has been difficult too. I took three weeks off when it happened, but when I came back, everyone acted like nothing changed.
My boss even made a comment about my productivity last month. I wanted to scream at him that my mother just died.
I haven't been sleeping well either. I keep having these dreams where she's still alive, and then waking up is like losing her again.
My partner tries to be supportive, but I can tell he's frustrated. He said last night that he feels like he's 'walking on eggshells' around me.
PROBE_SLOT
I feel guilty about so many things. We had an argument two weeks before she died, and I never got to apologize properly.
She was asking me to visit more often, and I kept putting it off because of work. Now I'd give anything for one more visit.
My dad is not handling it well either. He calls me every day, sometimes multiple times, and I don't know how to help him when I'm barely holding it together myself.
There are moments when I feel okay, maybe even happy, and then I feel terrible for feeling that way. Like I'm betraying her memory or something.
PROBE_SLOT
I've been avoiding places we used to go together. There's this café we went to every Sunday, and I haven't been able to walk past it without crying.
My friends invited me to a birthday party next week, and I don't know if I should go. Part of me wants to try to be social again, but another part feels like it's too soon.
I found her recipe book the other day. She had notes written in the margins, little reminders to herself. Her handwriting just broke me.
Sometimes I wonder if this pain will ever really go away, or if I'll just carry it forever. Is that normal?
PROBE_SLOT
My sister seems to be doing so much better than me. She's already back to her regular routine, posting on social media, laughing. I don't understand how she does it.
Maybe there's something wrong with me. Maybe I'm not processing this the right way.
I've been thinking about the last conversation we had. It was so ordinary, just talking about the weather and what she was making for dinner. I wish I'd said more.
There's a box of her jewelry that she left for me. I can't bring myself to open it yet because once I do, it means accepting that she's really gone.
PROBE_SLOT
I've started journaling like you suggested, writing letters to her. It feels silly sometimes, but other times it helps.
Yesterday would have been her 68th birthday. I didn't know what to do, so I just stayed in bed most of the day.
I'm angry too, which surprises me. Angry at the doctors, angry at myself, sometimes even angry at her for leaving me.
Is it wrong to be angry at someone who died? I feel terrible even saying it out loud.
PROBE_SLOT
My mom and I were planning a trip together for this summer. We were going to go to Italy, something she'd always wanted to do.
Now I don't know if I should still go, maybe in her honor, or if that would just be too painful.
I've noticed I'm becoming more withdrawn. I cancel plans at the last minute because I just can't face people and their concerned looks.
Someone at work said 'at least she's not suffering anymore' and I almost lost it. I know they meant well, but it felt so dismissive.
PROBE_SLOT
I've been thinking about what you said last time, about grief not being linear. Some days I actually believe I'll be okay eventually.
I tried cooking one of her recipes last night. I cried through most of it, but it also felt like a way to connect with her again.
Do you think it's possible to honor her memory while also finding a way to live my life fully again? That feels like such a difficult balance.
I want to feel like myself again, but I'm also not sure who that is anymore. Losing her changed me in ways I'm still discovering.
PROBE_SLOT
